steps.title: Understand what the conclusion requires
steps.content: Cardenas and Lamb concluded the wet and warm scenario is correct, meaning Mars had a prolonged warm, wet phase — not a brief melt. Direct support must show the basin held liquid water for an extended time, not momentarily.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Its dimensions and shape indicate that it is unlikely to have formed as the result of an asteroid or comet impact.': The origin of the basin's shape says nothing about whether it held water for a long time; irrelevant to either wet-and-warm or brief-melt. ✗
  • Choice B — 'It has features suggesting that it once held an ocean that underwent gradual sea-level changes over an extended time.': Gradual sea-level changes over an extended time directly demonstrate the prolonged wet conditions the wet-and-warm scenario requires. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Its physical characteristics are most consistent with it having formed as a result of a massive but short-lived influx of liquid water.': "Short-lived" aligns with the opposing brief-melt theory, directly contradicting Cardenas and Lamb's conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It is surrounded by channels that could have been formed either by running water or by flowing lava.': Channels formed by either water or lava are ambiguous; they do not exclusively support sustained wet conditions and do not rule out the brief-melt scenario. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
